title: Chapter 15: The War Council
content: Our first strategy session happened that night, around the kitchen table of Apartment 12B, with a single battery lantern casting shadows across five faces—well, four and a half, since Boss was lying across the table like he owned it.

"The situation," I began, channeling every military briefing I'd ever watched in movies, "is this. We have one secure location: this apartment, reinforced with a titanium door. We have limited supplies—maybe two weeks of food if we ration aggressively. We have one hostile faction on Floor 8: Derek Voss, plus two recruits, armed with a 9mm pistol and bladed weapons. And we have an unknown number of zombies throughout the building, concentrated on the lower floors."

"Threat assessment," Mia said, her rifle propped against the wall. "Derek's gun has, what, six rounds? He fired two during the stairwell chase. That leaves four, assuming he hasn't found more ammo. His recruits—Rick and Maria—are civilians. He probably gave the knife to the man and nothing to the woman."
"How do you know their names?" Sarah asked.

"I told you. I've been watching." Mia's tone was flat, clinical. "Rick is mid-thirties, former retail—probably ended up with Derek because he's too scared to be alone. Maria is early twenties, very scared, hasn't left 8A since she arrived. Derek keeps her on a short leash."
"Another woman he's controlling," Sarah said quietly.
"Another woman we can't save until we deal with Derek," Mia corrected. "Priorities. We take out the head, the body follows."
"Take out," I repeated. "You mean—"

"I mean neutralize. Kill, if it comes to that. Capture, if it's practical. But Derek Voss is not going to stop because we ask nicely. He's going to keep coming until one of us is dead, and he's got a head start on the ruthlessness department."

The word *kill* hung in the air like smoke. I'd never killed anyone. The closest I'd come was the zombie on the stairwell that Luna had decapitated—and that hadn't felt like killing, just... cleaning up. Derek was a person. A terrible person, but a person.
"I'm not a killer," I said.

"Neither am I," Mia said. "But I've been shooting targets since I was twelve, and the targets don't shoot back. Derek does. If he aims that gun at you or at Luna or at anyone in this room, I'm putting him down. That's not murder. That's survival."

Sarah spoke up. "He won't stop. I know him. I've been inside his head for a week—it's a bad neighborhood. He'll wait. He'll watch. He'll find a weakness, and he'll exploit it. The only language Derek understands is force."
Jessica hadn't spoken since the meeting started. She was sitting in the corner, hugging her knees, watching the discussion with wide eyes. But now she said, barely above a whisper:
"He hurts people because he likes it."
Simple. True. Devastating.

I looked around the table—at my people, my strange, mismatched, impossible family. A recovering zombie. A pre-med student with bruised arms. a teenage girl with trauma in her eyes. A competitive shooter who'd spent nine days alone on a roof. A cat.

None of them were soldiers. None of them wanted war. But war had come to them anyway, wearing Derek Voss's face, and the choice wasn't between fighting and peace. It was between fighting and extinction.
"Okay," I said. "Here's the plan."
The plan had three phases.

**Phase One: Intel.** Mia would continue her rooftop surveillance, mapping Derek's movements, counting his supplies, and identifying weaknesses. Luna would conduct floor-by-floor sweeps, clearing zombies from the stairwells between Floor 8 and Floor 12 to establish a safe travel corridor. I would use the system's daily quests to stockpile food and medical supplies.

**Phase Two: Isolation.** We would cut Derek off from external resources. The vending machines on Floors 7 and 8 were his primary supply source—I'd already raided Floor 8, but Floor 7 was still stocked. If we emptied or destroyed those machines, Derek would be forced to range further for supplies, which meant leaving his fortress. And a mobile Derek was a vulnerable Derek.

**Phase Three: Confrontation.** When the moment was right—when Derek was isolated, under-supplied, and predictable—we would force a decisive engagement. The specifics would depend on circumstances, but the goal was clear: remove Derek as a threat, rescue Maria, and secure Floor 8 as our expanded territory.
"Three phases," Mia said, reviewing my hand-drawn map of the building. "Sound strategy. Linear, but adaptable. What's the timeline?"
"Week to ten days for Phase One. Another week for Phase Two. Phase Three when opportunity presents."
"Could be faster."
"Could also be a disaster. Slow is smooth, smooth is fast."
She raised an eyebrow. "That's a Ranger saying."
"It's a Call of Duty loading screen tip. Same thing."
That almost-smile again. Mia Santos was starting to grow on me—like a cactus, beautiful from a distance but covered in spikes that would draw blood if you got too close.

**QUEST UPDATE: SARAH MITCHELL**
**AFFECTION: 28%**
**NOTE: Collaborative planning increases trust. Shared vulnerability accelerates bonding. Continue authentic engagement.**
The system was coaching me. In the middle of a war council, with lives on the line, it was taking notes on my love life like a wingman with a clipboard.
I ignored it. The system could track my numbers. It couldn't track my priorities, and right now, priority one was keeping everyone alive.
"Anything else?" I asked.
Luna raised her hand. It was such a human gesture—something she'd learned from watching me—that it made my chest ache.
"Dead things," she said. "Changing."
"Changing how?"
"Smarter. On lower floors. One watched me. Yesterday. Not shambling. Standing. *Watching*." She pointed at the window. "Like people used to watch. Like... waiting."
A chill ran through the room. The zombies were evolving. I'd read the system notification about "evolved variants," but we hadn't seen evidence until now.
"Is it dangerous?" Sarah asked.
"Unknown," Luna admitted. "But it was... aware. More than the others. It saw me and did not attack. It *studied* me."
The implications were terrible. Zombies that thought. Zombies that planned. Zombies that *waited*.

"Add it to the threat board," I said, grabbing a pen and writing *EVOLVED ZOMBIES — FLOOR UNKNOWN* in big red letters on my map. "We deal with Derek first. Then we figure out what's happening to the dead."

The meeting adjourned. Jessica went to sleep on the couch with Boss. Sarah took the window watch—her preferred post, where she could see the courtyard and not feel trapped. Mia returned to the roof with a promise to report at dawn.
And Luna followed me to the bedroom, as she always did, her hand finding mine in the darkness.
"Scared?" she asked.
"Terrified."
"Good," she said, pressing close, her warmth a constant surprise against my side. "Scared people are careful. Careful people survive."
"When did you get so wise?"

"I was always wise. Just couldn't talk." She paused, and in the dark I felt her smile—a rare and precious thing, the curve of her lips against my shoulder. "Now I can talk. So I say: we will win. Together."
"Together," I agreed.

She fell asleep first—zombie sleep was deep and sudden, like someone flipping a switch. I lay awake a while longer, listening to the building breathe, thinking about evolved zombies and angry men with guns and the terrifying, wonderful, impossible fact that I wasn't alone anymore.
The watch on my wrist pulsed once. A soft blue glow.
**SYSTEM TIP: Rest well, Host. Tomorrow, the real work begins.**
For once, the system didn't feel like a leash.
It felt like a promise.
